title: Dog Eyelid Growth Rubbing the Cornea: Symptoms and Care Guide
meta_desc: Discover what causes dog eyelid growths, why corneal irritation is a concern, and the urgency of veterinary care for eyelid masses.
content_html:
What is this growth on my dog's eyelid?

Growths on a dog's eyelid are common, especially as they age. Most often, these are Meibomian gland tumors, which are typically benign but can grow large enough to irritate the eye. Other possibilities include eyelid cysts, papillomas (warts), or in some cases, more aggressive tumors. Regardless of the type, when a mass is located on the eyelid margin, it can act like sandpaper against the sensitive surface of the eye.

Is this a veterinary emergency?

This situation is considered a Medium Urgency level. While the growth itself may not be an immediate life threat, the fact that it is rubbing against the cornea is a significant concern. Constant friction can lead to corneal ulcers, which are painful and can quickly become infected, potentially risking your dog's vision. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours.

Why a photo helps with triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of the eyelid growth helps veterinary professionals assess the size, texture, and exact position of the mass. A photo allows a vet to see if there is already visible inflammation or cloudiness on the cornea, which helps them determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and helps in planning for potential surgical removal.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter eye drops for the growth?
faqs.answer: You should never use human eye drops or medications on your dog without a vet's approval, as some ingredients can worsen a corneal ulcer.

faqs.question: Will the eyelid growth go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Most eyelid masses in dogs do not resolve on their own and tend to grow slowly over time, increasing the risk of eye damage.

faqs.question: What are signs that the eye is becoming damaged?
faqs.answer: Watch for excessive squinting, redness in the white of the eye, yellow or green discharge, or a cloudy appearance on the surface of the eyeball.
